metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either tighten `add-regression-test`'s `applies_when` to exclude the change-detector-test context, or add a note that AC3 is a prompt-level instruction (not a mechanical constraint) and adjust AC3's wording to reflect that the mechanism offers the right move but cannot exclude competing moves from the applicable set.
findings.finding: AC3 states 'A change-detector-test finding maps to delete-bad-test, not an improve-the-test move,' but the existing `add-regression-test` move has `applies_when: ["tests", "always"]`, making it always applicable — both it and the new `delete-bad-test` would appear in the applicable set simultaneously, and the kernel mechanism only controls which moves are offered, not which the LLM picks. The 'maps to' guarantee is a selection expectation, not a mechanically enforced invariant.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the enforcement mechanism for AC3's mutual-exclusion requirement — e.g., a coach_listing instruction that explicitly names the delete-not-improve rule, a distinct applies_when tag for change-detector findings, or a note in the move template that pre-empts the 'improve' alternative.
findings.finding: AC3 asserts 'A change-detector-test finding maps to delete-bad-test, not an improve the test move', implying a structural exclusion rule, but the plan provides no mechanism for this mutual-exclusion routing. The applicable_moves filter is purely additive (intersection-based): if 'delete-bad-test' and 'add-regression-test' both carry applies_when=['tests'], both appear in the LLM's choice set for any tests-tagged finding, and the LLM can still pick add-regression-test for a change-detector finding — violating AC3.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria, AC3: 'A change-detector-test finding maps to delete-bad-test, not an improve the test move'

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a testing plan covering: (a) applies_when gate happy-path and negative-path (wrong tag → move not offered), (b) subject validation rejects empty/malformed input, and (c) a behavioral assertion that a change-detector-test finding selects delete-bad-test and not any 'improve the test' move.
findings.finding: The plan introduces new conditional logic (applies_when gating, subject validation, move-routing rule) but provides no testing plan — no happy-path, failure-path, or contract tests are described for any of the three acceptance criteria.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria / Scope (moves.py + coach template set)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a test AC such as: 'Unit tests assert that a finding tagged change-detector-test resolves to delete-bad-test (not improve-test), that a finding without a matching applies_when tag returns no move, and that subject validation raises on empty input.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify which applies_when tag(s) each new move will use (must be drawn from OVERLAY_IDS ∪ 'always'), and confirm that at least one surviving-finding path emits a matching criteria tag so the moves are reachable by applicable_moves().
findings.finding: Neither 'file-follow-on-ticket' nor 'delete-bad-test' exist anywhere in the codebase. The plan treats them as additions to MOVE_REGISTRY in moves.py, but no stub, placeholder, or prior implementation is present. The plan's claim that these moves can be added with 'appropriate applies_when tags' relies on the closed OVERLAY_IDS vocabulary ({security, performance, i18n, a11y, db-migrations, docs, supply-chain, api-compat, iac, tests, llm-prompts} ∪ 'always'), yet the plan never names which tags will gate each move. An applies_when tag not in this set is accepted by validate_move_registry (it only checks string type, not enum membership), but the gate's applicability filter will never fire it unless a surviving finding carries a matching criteria tag. If 'file-follow-on-ticket' uses an ad-hoc tag not emitted by any reviewer, it will never be offered.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — AC1 ('file-follow-on-ticket and delete-bad-test moves exist with deterministic templates') and AC2 ('gated by appropriate applies_when tags')
findings.suggested_fix: For 'delete-bad-test': use applies_when: ['tests'] (the 'tests' OVERLAY_ID is the natural trigger, and the tests overlay covers change-detector findings). For 'file-follow-on-ticket': use applies_when: [] or ['always'] since pre-existing issues can arise in any overlay context — then document this choice explicitly in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact applies_when tag(s) for each new move using only OVERLAY_IDS vocabulary (e.g., delete-bad-test → ['tests']; file-follow-on-ticket → ['always'] or explain the intended trigger).
findings.finding: The plan states both new moves are 'gated by appropriate applies_when tags' but does not specify which tags from the closed OVERLAY_IDS vocabulary each move will use. For delete-bad-test, 'tests' is the natural tag; for file-follow-on-ticket there is no matching overlay ID — it may require 'always', which makes it applicable on every surviving finding regardless of criteria. The kernel's applicable_moves() filters moves against the union of surviving findings' criteria, so an incorrect or missing tag silently changes move applicability at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix: delete-bad-test: applies_when: ["tests"]. file-follow-on-ticket: applies_when: ["always"] (pre-existing issues can arise in any overlay's findings, so 'always' is the broadest correct choice; document the rationale).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `applies_when` tag for `file-follow-on-ticket` and explain how the closed `OVERLAY_IDS ∪ {"always"}` vocabulary can correctly gate a cross-cutting 'pre-existing issue' concern — or document that `"always"` is intentional and that the LLM's role is solely to not pick it for non-pre-existing findings.
findings.finding: The `applies_when` tag for `file-follow-on-ticket` is unspecified, and no tag in the closed `OVERLAY_IDS ∪ {"always"}` vocabulary semantically represents 'this finding concerns a pre-existing issue.' Pre-existing findings span all overlays, so any single overlay tag gates incorrectly; 'always' over-offers the move for non-pre-existing findings; the plan leaves this unresolved.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one concrete proving command or test reference per AC (e.g. `pytest tests/test_moves.py::test_delete_bad_test_routing` for AC3, a fixture-driven render check for AC1) so done-ness is verifiable without invention.
findings.finding: No proving command or test reference exists for any acceptance criterion — an executing agent cannot verify done-ness without stopping to invent a verification approach.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'appropriate applies_when tags' in AC2 with the specific tag names/values that must be set for each move (e.g. `applies_when: pre_existing_issue` for file-follow-on-ticket and `applies_when: change_detector_test` for delete-bad-test).
findings.finding: AC2 uses 'appropriate applies_when tags' — 'appropriate' is a subjective placeholder that defers the actual tag-value decision to the executor.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'gated by appropriate applies_when tags'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify the specific file(s) that constitute the 'coach template set' edit target (e.g., src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code-review-coach.md, a langfuse prompt, or confirm no template file change is needed since moves are injected dynamically).
findings.finding: The plan names 'the coach template set' as a second edit target alongside moves.py, but does not identify any specific file(s) that constitute this target. The code-review coach template (src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code-review-coach.md) exists but is move-list-agnostic (moves are injected dynamically at runtime via workflow_ops). It is unclear whether any template file actually requires editing, or if the plan is referring to a langfuse-hosted prompt not present in this repo.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief ADR or CLAUDE.md entry capturing the two-move routing contract (pre-existing issues → file-follow-on-ticket with discovered_from; change-detector tests → delete-bad-test, never re-pin) so future contributors understand the design intent without reading the original ticket.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new routing contract (pre-existing issues → file-follow-on-ticket; change-detector tests → delete-bad-test, not improve) but does not mention capturing this design decision in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc update, leaving future contributors without a recorded rationale for why these two cases are handled differently from other findings.
findings.location: ## Why / ## What (plan-level; no doc-update step in Scope or ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section to CLAUDE.md (or a lightweight ADR) under 'Coach move routing' that states: (1) pre-existing issues must use file-follow-on-ticket + discovered_from rather than inline feedback, and (2) change-detector/tautological tests must be deleted — an 'improve the test' move is explicitly ruled out.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale explaining why the move-catalog extension (vs. verdict-layer routing for pre-existing issues) is the right fit — e.g., lower blast radius, alignment with existing coach architecture, no verdict-path change required.
findings.finding: The plan states no positive rationale for why the move-catalog extension approach is chosen over alternatives (e.g., routing pre-existing-issue findings out of the coach pass entirely at the verdict-assembly layer, or adding a structured `pre_existing` flag to the finding contract that the workflow handles deterministically).
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the 'What' or 'Why' section: 'The move-catalog approach is chosen because it requires no verdict-path change, aligns with the existing coach architecture, and keeps the pre-existing-issue guidance in the same deterministic-template/subject-validation channel as all other moves.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'subject validation' concretely in AC2: specify which fields must be present, their types/constraints, and an example of a valid and invalid subject for each move.
findings.finding: 'subject validation' in AC2 is undefined — what fields are validated, what rules apply, and what a passing vs failing validation looks like are all unspecified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief description in the plan body (What or Scope) of what 'subject validation' means (e.g., non-empty string, bounded length, allowed characters) so AC2 is measurable.
findings.finding: AC2 references 'subject validation' as a verifiable criterion, but the plan body never describes what subject validation entails (schema, non-empty check, format constraint, etc.), making this acceptance criterion partially unmeasurable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2: 'Moves are gated by appropriate applies_when tags and pass subject validation.'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, add: 'Subject validation ensures the LLM-provided subject is a non-empty string within a defined length bound (e.g., 5–200 characters), rejecting blank or placeholder values.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (and document in the plan) whether the existing `applies_when` tag-evaluation machinery is fully generic (works for any new tag without code changes) or enumerate what additional wiring is needed for the two new tags.
findings.finding: The plan introduces new `applies_when` tags for both moves but does not acknowledge whether the existing tag-evaluation machinery in the coach pipeline already handles arbitrary new tags generically or requires an extension to recognize them.
findings.location: ## What / Acceptance Criteria line 2: 'Moves are gated by appropriate applies_when tags'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence under ## Scope: 'The applies_when evaluator is data-driven and requires no code changes for new tags' — OR add the evaluator file to ## Scope if it needs updating.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact `applies_when` tag(s) for each new move using only values from `{OVERLAY_IDS ∪ "always"}`; confirm that `delete-bad-test` uses `["tests"]` (or `["always"]` with justification) and `file-follow-on-ticket` uses `["always"]` (or a specific overlay subset), so the plan can be verified against `test_code_review_ws2.py:78-83`.
findings.finding: The plan says both moves are 'gated by appropriate applies_when tags' but doesn't specify which tags from the CLOSED `{OVERLAY_IDS ∪ "always"}` vocabulary each move will use. `validate_move_registry` (strict=True for built-ins) raises ValueError at load for any tag outside that set, and `test_code_review_ws2.py:78-83` enforces this. `delete-bad-test` might naively use a tag like `change-detector` or `tautological` that doesn't exist in OVERLAY_IDS, causing a load-time crash. `file-follow-on-ticket` has no obvious single-overlay trigger. Without specifying the tags, the plan cannot be verified against the closed-vocabulary contract.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Moves are gated by appropriate applies_when tags and pass subject validation.' / src/rebar/llm/code_review/moves.py MOVE_REGISTRY
findings.suggested_fix: For `delete-bad-test`: `"applies_when": ["tests"]` — it applies when the tests overlay is active (a change-detector test is a test-sufficiency finding). For `file-follow-on-ticket`: `"applies_when": ["always"]` — pre-existing issues can surface in any overlay, so always-applicable is correct.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact applies_when tag(s) for both 'file-follow-on-ticket' and 'delete-bad-test' drawn explicitly from OVERLAY_IDS ∪ {always}, or justify adding a new tag id to OVERLAY_IDS (with the attendant drift-guard test update).
findings.finding: The plan says both new moves are 'gated by appropriate applies_when tags' but never specifies which tag values from the closed OVERLAY_IDS ∪ {always} vocabulary each move will carry. For 'file-follow-on-ticket' in particular, none of the 11 OVERLAY_IDS (security, performance, i18n, a11y, db-migrations, docs, supply-chain, api-compat, iac, tests, llm-prompts) corresponds to 'pre-existing issue' — the executor must invent a tag without guidance, and the existing load-time validation (test_code_review_ws2.py line 78–84) will reject any tag outside the closed set.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria, AC2: 'Moves are gated by appropriate applies_when tags'
findings.suggested_fix: For 'delete-bad-test': applies_when=['tests'] (the 'tests' overlay already covers test-quality findings). For 'file-follow-on-ticket': applies_when=['always'] (pre-existing issues can surface in any overlay context), with an explicit call-out in the plan that 'always' is intentional because pre-existing issues are cross-cutting.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
